hallucinations.description: Multiple citations reference 2025 arXiv preprints (e.g., Li & Song 2025 arXiv:2509.12179, Siu et al. 2025 arXiv:2503.15516, Neto et al. 2025 arXiv:2512.20621) that do not correspond to verifiable existing papers.
hallucinations.evidence: References section: 'Li, Y., & Song, W. (2025). Co-Alignment: Rethinking Alignment as Bidirectional Human-AI Cognitive Adaptation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.12179.' and similar entries for other 2025 works.

hallucinations.description: The paper frames ~11% accuracy (near-random for 10 actions) as successful empirical validation of bidirectional alignment with 'up to 10.6% improvement', while hiding that all models perform at chance level and adaptation metrics contain numerical artifacts (e.g., 160000 values from division issues).
hallucinations.evidence: Section 5 tables and abstract claim positive results; log.txt and evaluation.py show accuracies of 0.10-0.126 and exploding adaptation_accuracy values never mentioned in paper.

overall_assessment: Hallucinations found: nonexistent citations and misleading presentation of near-random experimental results as validation of the proposed framework.
